rules.description: Commit directly to main/current branch, no new branches

action.alternative: Only create branch if user explicitly requests

rules.rationale: Simplified workflow for prototyping, user manages branching strategy

rules.description: Never force push to main/master without explicit request

action.message: Force push to main destructive. Warn user if requested.

rules.rationale: Prevent history rewriting on shared branches, data loss prevention

examples.scenario: Push fails due to diverged history
examples.correct: Explain conflict, suggest pull or ask user how to proceed

rules.description: Never use --no-verify or --no-gpg-sign unless explicitly requested

action.alternative: Let hooks run, fix issues if they fail

rules.rationale: Hooks enforce quality/security standards, bypassing defeats purpose

rules.description: Never create commits unless user explicitly requests

action.message: Only commit when user explicitly asks

rules.rationale: User controls commit timing, may want to review/test first

examples.correct: Mention work complete, wait for user to request commit
examples.incorrect: Automatically run git add && git commit

rules.description: Use HEREDOC for git commit messages
rules.rationale: Ensures proper formatting, handles multi-line messages, avoids quote escaping

examples.incorrect: git commit -m 'feat: Add auth' (quote issues if message complex)

rules.rationale: Amending others' commits rewrites their history, collaboration issue

examples.scenario: Need to amend commit after pre-commit hook changes
examples.correct: Check author matches current user, then amend

rules.description: Retry git push/pull/fetch on network failures (4x exponential backoff)

unless: non-network error like conflict, auth failure

rules.rationale: Transient network failures common, auto-retry improves success rate

examples.scenario: git push fails with network timeout
examples.correct: Wait 2s, retry. If fails, wait 4s, retry. Continue up to 4 attempts.
examples.incorrect: Immediately give up after first network failure
